retrieved_docs.title: Tumor-Suppressor Genes -- Clinical Significance -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: PTCH1: PTCH1 tumor suppressor gene encodes protein patched homolog 1 that negatively regulated the hedgehog signaling pathway. [36] [37] Gorlin syndrome is an autosomal dominant disorder that correlates with a germline loss-of-function mutation in this gene and has a high risk of developing basal cell carcinoma and medulloblastoma. Sporadic cases of basal cell carcinoma and medulloblastomas are frequently associated with acquired biallelic loss-of-function PTCH1 mutations. [36]

retrieved_docs.title: Basal Cell Carcinoma -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Literature suggests that the cells of origin from which BCC arises are immature, pluripotent cells associated with the hair follicle. Of note, the gene most often altered in BCCs is the PTCH1 gene. PTCH1 gene mutations occur in 70% of people with sporadic BCC. Ten percent to 20% of people with sporadic BCC have smoothened ( SMO) mutations. Literature suggests that a sufficiently elevated expression level of Gli , by activating mutations of SMO or by homozygous inactivation of PTCH1 in a responding cell, is sufficient to drive the formation of BCC. The second most common mutation found in BCCs is in the P53 gene. Mutations in CDKN2A locus also have been detected in a smaller number of sporadic BCCs.

retrieved_docs.title: Retinoblastoma -- Differential Diagnosis -- Approach to Differentiating Retinoblastoma from Similar Clinical Entities
retrieved_docs.content: Genetic testing plays a key role in confirming hereditary retinoblastoma by identifying mutations in the RB1 gene. Laboratory tests, including specific serologies (such as Toxocara antibodies) and inflammatory markers, help rule out infections or autoimmune conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: Medulloblastoma -- Staging
retrieved_docs.content: Using a combination of molecular profiling and histology, the World Health Organization (WHO) presently divides medulloblastoma into five subtypes [37] : WNT-activated with classic histology SHH-activated (wild-type TP53), with classic, desmoplastic/nodular, or large-cell/anaplastic histology SHH-activated (mutant TP53), with classic or large-cell/anaplastic histology non-WNT/non-SHH, group 3, with classic or large-cell anaplastic histology non-WNT/non-SHH, group 4, with classic or large-cell/anaplastic histology

retrieved_docs.title: Malignant Eyelid Lesions -- Pathophysiology -- Basal Cell Carcinoma
retrieved_docs.content: UV radiation and smoking have been implicated as the major risk factor for developing BCC due to DNA damage. [49] Several studies have identified mutations in the PTCH1 gene (of the sonic hedgehog pathway) and p53 tumor suppressor gene in patients with BCC. The p53 tumor suppressor gene is critical to cell apoptosis and the prevention of tumorigenesis; a mutation in this gene leads to the uncontrolled growth of malignant cells. [50] [51]

retrieved_docs.title: Thyroid Cancer -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Anaplastic - Inactivating mutation of the p53 tumor suppressor gene has been identified in addition to early inactivating mutations in about 50 to 80% of the cases with anaplastic thyroid cancer. [8] [9] [10] Also, 66% of anaplastic thyroid cancers have been identified to harbor mutations in the CTNNB1 gene. [5] RAS mutations are also associated with 20 to 40% of anaplastic thyroid cancers. MTC - Germline mutations of RET proto-oncogene in inherited forms of MTC (approximately 25% of MTC) and RAS mutations in 25% of MTC. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Gorlin Syndrome -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Gorlin syndrome is caused by a mutation in patched 1 ( PTCH1 ), a tumor suppressor gene located on chromosome 9q. PTCH encodes a transmembrane receptor protein that recognizes signaling proteins of the sonic hedgehog family. Homozygous inactivation of the PTCH gene leads to tumorigenecity and the formation of multiple BCCs and other neoplasms. Patients with Gorlin syndrome inherit one defective copy of the tumor suppressor gene and acquire a "second hit" mutation, such as from ultraviolet light or ionizing radiation. Recently, mutations in suppressor of fused gene ( SUFU ) on chromosome 10q and PTCH2 on chromosome 1p have been found in patients meeting criteria for Gorlin's syndrome. Of note, patients with SUFU mutations have a reported 20-fold increased risk of developing medulloblastoma as compared to PTCH1 mutations in Gorlin syndrome. [5] [6] [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Skin Cancer -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The development of cutaneous malignancy following DNA damage from solar radiation is multifactorial and influenced by genetic factors, Fitzpatrick skin type, and immunosuppressed status. Most (90%) cutaneous squamous cell carcinomas exhibit UV-induced p53 gene mutations, leading to uninhibited keratinocyte proliferation. [48] DNA mutations associated with basal cell carcinoma include mutations in the PTCH and p53 genes. [49] Melanoma-related DNA mutations include alterations in CDKN2A, MCR1, BRAF , and DNA repair enzymes, such as UV-specific endonuclease in xeroderma pigmentosum. [50] [51] The complete list of genetic mutations and risk factors remains incomplete, but ongoing research continues to uncover new genes and targets involved in each subtype of skin cancer. [52] [53] [54]

retrieved_docs.title: Tumor-Suppressor Genes -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: In the inherited form of retinoblastoma, children inherit one mutated RB allele (germ-line mutation), and the other copy is normal. Retinoblastoma develops when the normal retinoblastoma ( RB) allele undergoes a spontaneous somatic mutation; this is the “second hit.” In sporadic cases of retinoblastoma, both normal RB alleles must undergo a somatic mutation in the same cell. The probability of this is low, which explains why retinoblastoma is uncommon in the general population. In both cases, the resulting two hits lead to the development of retinoblastoma. [9] Based on these original observations, there are three important properties of classic tumor suppressor genes (TSGs):

retrieved_docs.title: Anaplastic Thyroid Cancer -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Mutations in the TERT promoter region and TP53 are common, with TERT promoter mutations associated with RAS and BRAF mutations. Other targetable mutations may be seen, including NTRK and ALK rearrangements. [9] Thyroid-specific rearrangements RET/PTC and PAX8/PPARγ are rarely found in poorly differentiated or undifferentiated thyroid cancer, suggesting that these genetic alterations do not predispose cells to dedifferentiation. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Goiter -- Pathophysiology -- Pathophysiology of Nodular Goiter
retrieved_docs.content: Radiation: Accidental or medical radiation, by external ionizing radiation or radioactive iodine, causes thyroid abnormalities. Individuals exposed to radiation under the age of 20 years have an increase in the incidence of thyroid nodules in early years and papillary thyroid cancer as they age. Genetic factors, mutations: SPOP , ZNF148, and EZH1 in benign nodules and PAX8/PPAR g , RET/PTC , BRAF, and RAS in papillary thyroid cancer.

retrieved_docs.title: Retinoblastoma -- Evaluation -- Genetic Testing
retrieved_docs.content: RB1 mutation analysis is essential for detecting germline or somatic mutations in the RB1 gene. This analysis is critical for distinguishing hereditary from sporadic retinoblastoma and helping identify at-risk family members, enabling targeted surveillance for potential secondary cancers. [96]

retrieved_docs.title: Skin Cancer -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The etiology of skin cancer varies depending on the type of cancer. UV radiation is a major etiologic factor for most nonmelanoma skin cancers, including squamous cell carcinoma and basal cell carcinoma, as well as melanoma. [6] [7] [8] Along with chemical carcinogens and wound-related changes, UV radiation is believed to damage DNA, which results in mutations in tumor suppressor genes (eg, p53 ) and genomic instability. [9] [10] [11] Most cutaneous squamous cell carcinomas (90%) exhibit UV-induced p53 gene mutations, leading to the uncontrolled proliferation of keratinocytes. [12] Mutations implicated in basal cell carcinoma include mutations in the PTCH gene and the p53 gene. [13] Mutations associated with melanoma include alterations in cyclin-dependent kinase inhibitor-2A (CDKN2A), melanocortin-1 receptor (MC1R), BRAF , and DNA repair enzymes, such as UV-specific endonuclease in xeroderma pigmentosum. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Thyroid Cancer -- Evaluation -- Role of Molecular Testing in Thyroid Cancer
retrieved_docs.content: Gene mutation profiling panel- such as the 7-gene panel that detects multiple genes including BRAF V600E, HRAS codon 61, KRAS codons 12/13, and NRAS codon 61 point mutations, and RET/PTC1, PAX8/PPARγ, and RET/PTC3 translocations which account for approximately 70% of thyroid cancer. [41] This panel has improved the sensitivity and the negative predictive of these tests to >90%. [42] Therefore, mutation tests are good rule-in tests for thyroid cancer. [43] A 167-Gene expression classifier provides a strong negative predictive value, while its positive predictive value is only around 50%. [41] Hence, gene expression classifier testing is a good rule-out test for thyroid cancer. However, recently a large multi-gene panel of mutation markers has been introduced, further improving sensitivity and specificity. [43] [44]

retrieved_docs.title: Tumor-Suppressor Genes -- Clinical Significance -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: Many tumor suppressor genes have been the object of studies, and there are likely many more that have yet to be discovered. The mechanisms of each tumor suppressor gene and its protein products are complex and interrelated to other cell signaling pathways, but some better-known mechanisms appear below.

retrieved_docs.title: Papillary Thyroid Carcinoma -- Etiology -- Genetics
retrieved_docs.content: A few familial syndromes are associated with PTC, including familial adenomatous polyposis—Gardner syndrome, Werner syndrome, and Carney complex type 1. Familial PTC cases have been reported in 5% of all patients with PTC and may portend a more aggressive disease course. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2517
retrieved_docs.content: Cancer of thyroid is classified as a type of اضطراب صماوي, indicating that it belongs to the broader category of endocrine disorders. By recognizing thyroid cancer as an endocrine disorder, clinicians understand that the disease originates in the hormone‑producing thyroid gland and shares pathophysiological features with other conditions affecting the endocrine system. This classification guides diagnostic evaluation, treatment planning, and prognostic assessment within the context of endocrine pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2556
retrieved_docs.content: Primary malignant neoplasm of the nasal bone and primary malignant neoplasm of the thymus are both examples of primary malignant neoplastic proliferations, representing pathological processes in which cancerous cells originate and grow aggressively at their respective anatomical sites. As primary malignant neoplastic proliferations, each condition reflects a direct, localized malignant transformation of tissue—one affecting the nasal bone and the other affecting the thymus. Recognizing that both entities share the same fundamental nature as pathological processes of primary malignant neoplastic growth aids clinicians in understanding their aggressive behavior, guiding diagnostic evaluation, and informing treatment strategies tailored to the specific organ involved.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 1 -- Community 240
retrieved_docs.content: similarly defined as a primary malignant neoplastic proliferation. In addition, an inverse‑is‑a relationship links the entity “سرطانة توتية” to the primary malignant tumor morphology (“ورم خبيث أولي”), further emphasizing its classification as a type of primary malignant neoplasm. Together, these relationships illustrate how various primary malignant neoplasms across different organs are unified by common pathological processes, shared morphological descriptors, and hierarchical links to broader cancer concepts, aiding in consistent classification and clinical understanding.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2412
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to lymph nodes of the neck originating from a primary malignant neoplasm of the thyroid, primary malignant neoplasm of the head of the pancreas, and primary malignant neoplasm of the thymus are all instances of primary malignant neoplastic proliferations, which are pathological processes characterized by uncontrolled malignant cell growth. Each condition represents a distinct organ‑specific cancer that arises as a primary tumor (thyroid, pancreatic head, thymus) and, in the case of the thyroid cancer, can spread to regional lymph nodes in the neck. Recognizing these entities as primary malignant neoplastic proliferations highlights their shared nature as serious pathological processes that require comprehensive oncologic assessment, staging, and targeted therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.content: فالج is a general medical condition, and فالج طفلي represents a more specific, narrower form of فالج, indicating the infantile variant of this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 1039
retrieved_docs.content: Primary malignant neoplasm of the head of the pancreas and primary malignant neoplasm of the thymus are both classified as primary malignant neoplastic proliferations, which are pathological processes. As malignant tumors arising in distinct anatomical sites—the pancreatic head and the thymus—each represents an uncontrolled growth of neoplastic cells that characterizes a serious pathological condition. Recognizing these neoplasms as primary malignant neoplastic proliferations emphasizes their role as aggressive cancerous processes that require prompt diagnostic evaluation and targeted oncologic management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2179
retrieved_docs.content: Melanomas of skin are malignant neoplasms that arise from melanocytes in the cutaneous tissue and represent a specific form of skin cancer. سرطان الشفة الخبيث, which translates to malignant lip cancer, is a distinct malignant tumor that originates in the lip and is classified as an oral cavity cancer. Although both conditions are cancers, they affect different anatomical sites and are not subtypes of one another; each requires separate diagnostic and therapeutic approaches tailored to its location and biological behavior. Understanding the differences between melanomas of skin and سرطان الشفة الخبيث is important for accurate diagnosis, appropriate treatment planning, and effective patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2549
retrieved_docs.content: The term استئصال الدرقية is the Arabic name for thyroidectomy, a surgical procedure that involves removing all or part of the thyroid gland; it may be performed as a partial or a complete thyroidectomy depending on the underlying thyroid condition being treated.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2701
retrieved_docs.content: Primary malignant neoplasm of the head of the pancreas, primary malignant neoplasm of the nasal bone, and primary malignant neoplasm of the thymus are each examples of primary malignant neoplastic proliferations, which constitute pathological processes. These entities represent malignant tumor growth arising in distinct anatomical sites—the pancreatic head, the nasal bone, and the thymus—characterized by uncontrolled proliferation of cancerous cells. As pathological processes, they require oncologic assessment, appropriate staging, and targeted therapeutic strategies to manage the aggressive nature of the malignancy in each location.
